splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the timing rules for selecting actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability under ERISA's multiemployer pension plan provisions. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing of assumption selection directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that plan actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be fixed by the end of the prior plan year (the statutory measurement date), providing employers with greater predictability about potential liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts can apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises fundamental questions about judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, so the clock stops running and the court retains jurisdiction to revoke supervised release even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and the term expires according to its original duration regardless of the supervisee's compliance.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) from raising any claim they previously presented in a successive § 2255 motion, in addition to the separate requirements of § 2255(h). If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement; if not, they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement beyond § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, meaning they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations (Auer/Kisor deference) when construing ambiguous provisions in the Sentencing Guidelines that increase criminal liability.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts should defer to the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding the scope of criminal sentencing enhancements or instead apply the rule of lenity in favor of the defendant when a Guideline provision is ambiguous. The split has become more significant after Loper Bright overruled Chevron deference, though Auer/Kisor deference to agencies interpreting their own rules technically remains.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency deference when construing ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from using delegated power to expand criminal liability.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that deference to the Sentencing Commission's authoritative commentary controls over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions, even when the interpretation expands criminal liability.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) means defendants must meet all three criminal-history criteria to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether possessing any single criterion is disqualifying. This split leads to dramatically different sentences for defendants with identical criminal histories depending on which circuit they are in.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its plain conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any single criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' based on statutory context and structure.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' commentary (Application Note 1) can expand the definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy, when the guideline text does not mention them.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation entitled to deference under Stinson, or impermissibly expands the guideline text. This affects whether conspiracy convictions trigger the career-offender enhancement.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of § 4B1.2(b) entitled to deference under Stinson and Kisor, and prior conspiracy convictions qualify as career-offender predicates.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands the guideline
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline's scope beyond what the text allows, so conspiracy convictions do not qualify as predicates.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state controls a substance not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) is limited to substances controlled under federal law or encompasses substances controlled only under state law. This affects whether state convictions for substances not on federal schedules can trigger sentencing enhancements.

positions.label: State-only controlled substances qualify
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b) broadly defines 'controlled substance offense' to include state law offenses, even if the substance is not controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act, because there is no textual basis to graft a federal-law limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: Only federally-scheduled substances qualify
positions.summary: Holds that 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines requires the substance to be controlled under federal law, so state convictions involving substances not on the federal schedules cannot serve as predicates.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether non-retroactive changes in law can be considered as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar consideration of such changes, while others allow them to be considered alone or in combination with other factors. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to resolve this but has generated further disagreement about whether the Commission exceeded its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes in law cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in law, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons under § 3582(c)(1)(A), because allowing such consideration would circumvent Congress's decision not to make the change retroactive.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes in law may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as one factor among others in evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for compassionate release, consistent with the broad discretion granted by the statute.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging conditions or terms of their confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration of confinement) must proceed via habeas corpus or may use civil rights actions such as Bivens.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists over whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoners challenging conditions of confinement that do not directly contest the fact or duration of imprisonment, or whether they may also bring civil rights claims. This split has persisted for over half a century despite Supreme Court dicta in Preiser v. Rodriguez and subsequent cases acknowledging but not resolving the question.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy for confinement challenges
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to the terms and conditions of confinement, including BOP decisions about custody and placement, must be brought through habeas corpus and cannot be pursued through civil rights actions.

positions.label: Civil rights actions are available for conditions challenges
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners challenging conditions of confinement (rather than the fact or duration of imprisonment) are not limited to habeas corpus and may pursue civil rights claims.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal deadline for filing a notice of appeal applies to orders on Hyde Amendment motions for attorney's fees in criminal cases.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees, though filed in a criminal case, is a civil matter for purposes of determining the applicable appellate deadline. The majority view treats it as civil, applying the longer 30-day deadline rather than the 14-day criminal deadline, but at least one circuit has applied the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies rather than the 14-day criminal deadline.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that because the Hyde Amendment motion arises in and is part of the criminal case, the 14-day criminal appellate deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or the debtor. This affects whether trustees can seize the increased equity in a debtor's home for creditors after conversion, a question with significant practical consequences for debtors forced to convert due to changed circumstances.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that under the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A) and the broad scope of § 541(a), post-petition appreciation in property value inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor based on the structure, policies, and legislative history of the Bankruptcy Code, which was designed to protect good-faith converting debtors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release at revocation proceedings, or whether revocation sentences must be based solely on the breach of trust and the need to protect the public.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the permissible purposes of a revocation sentence imposed after a defendant violates supervised release. Some circuits allow courts to expressly punish for the violation conduct, while others hold that punishment for the new offense is not a permissible basis for a revocation sentence. The Sixth Circuit's internal precedent (United States v. Lewis) has been criticized as placing it at the extreme of this split.

positions.label: Courts may punish for violation conduct at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider and impose punishment for the underlying violation conduct when revoking supervised release and imposing a revocation sentence.

positions.label: Revocation sentences are not for punishing new conduct
positions.summary: Holds that the purpose of a supervised release revocation sentence is to sanction the breach of the court's trust and to protect the public, not to punish the defendant for the violation conduct itself, which should be addressed in a separate prosecution.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as an order effectively unreviewable on appeal from final judgment.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' that cannot be vindicated after disclosure. This potentially conflicts with circuits that take a narrower view of the collateral order doctrine's scope for sealing orders.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denials of motions to seal medical information satisfy the collateral order doctrine because maintaining medical privacy is a value of high order that is effectively lost once documents are publicly disclosed.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine, as protective orders and other mechanisms provide adequate alternative means to vindicate privacy interests short of immediate appeal.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the standard for evaluating whether candidates have pre-election standing to challenge voting procedures. The resolution affects how easily pre-election challenges to mail-in voting rules, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices can be brought.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge state election laws that allegedly dilute the value of lawfully cast votes or affect election outcomes.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's speculative allegations about the effect of an election law on vote counting are insufficient to establish the concrete injury required for Article III standing.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of liability and available remedies under 42 U.S.C. § 1981?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve a split in how circuits evaluate discrimination claims arising from the operation of business establishments. The Second Circuit's approach to the relevant legal questions was found sufficiently important to warrant review, suggesting a circuit split on applicable standards for liability and damages.

positions.label: Broader standard for discrimination claims
positions.summary: Holds that a broader set of legal standards applies to discrimination claims against hospitality or business establishments, allowing more expansive theories of liability and remedies.

positions.label: Narrower standard for discrimination claims
positions.summary: Holds that a more restrictive set of standards governs discrimination claims in the hospitality or business context, limiting available theories and remedies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption of state laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier' extends to state tort claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or selection of motor carriers. This affects the availability of tort remedies for injuries caused by trucking accidents arranged by freight brokers.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they relate to the broker's 'service' of arranging transportation, which is within the scope of FAAAA preemption.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent selection of carriers are not preempted because such claims do not sufficiently relate to the broker's prices, routes, or services within the meaning of the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus grounds — such as alleged errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence — may be considered in compassionate release motions. Some circuits allow such considerations as one factor, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for § 2255 habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider claimed legal errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot use compassionate release for habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for relitigating the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be brought under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
